coredata.dc:title: Dual therapy for the eradication of bacterial biofilms: Iron oxide nanoparticles and carbon dots as magnetic actuator and photothermal agents
coredata.dc:creator: Sahli, Célia
Deschamps, Julien
Royon, Laurent
Lomas, John S.
Briandet, Romain
Hémadi, Miryana
coredata.dc:description: Nanohybrids based on maghemite iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) and carbon dots (CDs), with different linkers between the two components, are synthesized, with the idea of combining several properties (magnetic and optical) in one nanomaterial in order to eradicate bacterial biofilm. The photothermal capacities of these materials are expressed by two parameters: the specific absorption rate (SAR) and the photothermal light-to-heat conversion constant (η). They show that the IONP/CD combination is more effective in photothermia (PT) than either of the components, but depends on the linkage (amide > ester > electrostatic). The antibacterial properties of the nanohybrids are first determined for the exponential and stationary growth phases of planktonic S. aureus and B. subtilis with and without PT. In the absence of PT, no nanohybrid has any significant bactericidal effect, but with PT the nanohybrids have different activities, with the IONP-amide-CD pattern the most effective. Combining magnetic actuation and PT on B. subtilis biofilms shows a synergistic effect and reveals the advantages of using such nanohybrid materials for killing bacteria and eradicating biofilm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron oxide nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Photothermia
Planktonic bacteria
Antimicrobial
Near-infrared laser

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antibacterial properties of nanohybrids on planktonic S. aureus and B. subtilis
Antibiofilm properties of NP-APTES-CD on B. subtilis biofilm

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ecole Chimie Physique et Chimie Analytique de Paris Centre

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Ecole Chimie Physique et Chimie Analytique de Paris Centre (ED388) of the Université Paris Cité for a research grant (for C.S.) and the ANR (Agence Nationale de la Recherche) and the CGI (Commissariat à l'Investissement d'Avenir) through IDEX and Labex SEAM (Science and Engineering for Advanced Materials and devices): ANR-18-IDEX-0001(MANO4T Emergence project), ANR 11 LABX 086 and ANR 11 IDEX 05 02, for financial support. The authors are grateful to Dr Philippe Decorse for the XPS spectra measurements on the XPS-UPS facility which is supported in part by the Ile-de-France regional council, SESAME agreement n° 16016303, and to Alexandre Chevillot-Biraud for the TGA. The authors are grateful to the French Antibiodeal network of the Promise PPR antibioresistance ANR program for stimulating scientific exchanges.

The authors thank the Ecole Chimie Physique et Chimie Analytique de Paris Centre (ED388) of the Université Paris Cité for a research grant (for C.S.) and the ANR ( Agence Nationale de la Recherche ) and the CGI ( Commissariat à l’Investissement d’Avenir ) through IDEX and Labex SEAM ( Science and Engineering for Advanced Materials and devices ): ANR-18-IDEX-0001 (MANO4T Emergence project), ANR 11 LABX 086 and ANR 11 IDEX 05 02 , for financial support. The authors are grateful to Dr Philippe Decorse for the XPS spectra measurements on the XPS-UPS facility which is supported in part by the Ile-de-France regional council , SESAME agreement n° 16016303 , and to Alexandre Chevillot-Biraud for the TGA. The authors are grateful to the French Antibiodeal network of the Promise PPR antibioresistance ANR program for stimulating scientific exchanges.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the device: the magnet, located under one well of the biofilm plate, is oscillated to induce a local displacement of the IONPs while the sample is irradiated from above by the laser.

Schematic pathways for synthesis of the different nanohybrids between IONPS and CDs: 1-covalent bonding between NPs and CDs by APTES linker; 2- covalent bonding by esterification; 3- direct coupling by electrostatic forces.

A: TEM of NP; DLS of CD, and size distribution. B: XPS survey spectra. C: FTIR. D: VSM: hysteresis loops recorded at 298 K. The inset represents a zoom of the coercive field of IONPs, NP-APTES, NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD.
Temperature versus time curves for continuous-wave laser irradiation of [Fe] = 20 mM nanohybrid suspensions (808 nm, power: 1.0 W) for 400 s.

Log(CFUs/mL) of S. aureus (A, B) and B. subtilis (C, D) either in the exponential A, C) or the stationary (B, D) growth phase, with or without PT (808 nm, power: 1.0 W). All experiments were performed in triplicate; bars indicate standard deviations.

Confocal microscope images (A) and mortality (B) of B. subtilis NDmed) biofilm in contact with IONP, NP-APTES or NP-APTES-CD, under magnetic actuation, PT or both. Bars indicate standard deviations (**: P < 0.01; ***: P < 0.001; ****: P < 0.0001).

Biovolume (in μm3) (A) and thickness (B) of B. subtilis biofilms when exposed to IONP, NP-APTES or NP-APTES-CD without treatment, with magnetic actuation, with PT and in dual magneto-photothermia. Associated confocal images are presented in Fig. 7A, and bars indicate standard deviations (***: P < 0.001; ****: P < 0.0001).
XPS analysis and Zeta potentials of the nanomaterials.
Organic content of the nanohybrids determined by Prussian Blue analysis, vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM), and TGA.
SAR values for bare IONPs and nanohybrids, with [Fe] = 5–100 mM.

head.ce:title: Dual therapy for the eradication of bacterial biofilms: Iron oxide nanoparticles and carbon dots as magnetic actuator and photothermal agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris Cité, CNRS-UMR 7086, ITODYS, 75013, Paris, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universite Paris Cite, CNRS-UMR 7086, ITODYS, 75013 Paris, France.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris-Saclay, INRAE, AgroParisTech, Micalis Institute, 78350, Jouy-en-Josas, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universite Paris-Saclay, INRAE, AgroParisTech, Micalis Institute, 78350 Jouy-en-Josas, France.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris Cité, CNRS-UMR 8236, Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Energies de Demain (LIED), 75013, Paris, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CNRS-UMR 8236, Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Energies de Demain (LIED)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universite Paris Cite, CNRS-UMR 8236, Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Energies de Demain (LIED), 75013 Paris, France.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 15 rue Jean-Antoine de Baïf, 75013, Paris, France.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanohybrids based on maghemite iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) and carbon dots (CDs), with different linkers between the two components, are synthesized, with the idea of combining several properties (magnetic and optical) in one nanomaterial in order to eradicate bacterial biofilm. The photothermal capacities of these materials are expressed by two parameters: the specific absorption rate (SAR) and the photothermal light-to-heat conversion constant (η). They show that the IONP/CD combination is more effective in photothermia (PT) than either of the components, but depends on the linkage (amide > ester > electrostatic). The antibacterial properties of the nanohybrids are first determined for the exponential and stationary growth phases of planktonic S. aureus and B. subtilis with and without PT. In the absence of PT, no nanohybrid has any significant bactericidal effect, but with PT the nanohybrids have different activities, with the IONP-amide-CD pattern the most effective. Combining magnetic actuation and PT on B. subtilis biofilms shows a synergistic effect and reveals the advantages of using such nanohybrid materials for killing bacteria and eradicating biofilm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based materials with a diameter less than 20 nm. They are sustainable nontoxic nanomaterials, synthesized from inexpensive precursors [1,2] with desirable optical properties such as photostability and photobleaching resistance. For these reasons they have potential applications in biosensors, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, fluorescent ink, drug delivery, photocatalysis and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [3,4]. The development of multifunctional hybrids in the field of biomedical applications is designed to take advantage of the synergy provided by coupling the several properties of one nanomaterial with those of another or others in order to obtain the material best adapted for a given application. One of the most interesting associations is that between CDs and inorganic materials such as iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs). Combining the excellent fluorescent response of CDs with the magnetism of IONPs results in multiple possible applications in many fields.

IONPs combined with CDs have applications as catalysts. Reduction of Pd(II) to Pd on magnetite NPs modified by CDs gave a nanohybrid which shows catalytic activity in Suzuki coupling reactions. The IONPs facilitate recovery of the catalyst for reuse [5]. In the field of biosensors and bioimaging, IONPs were combined with CDs by ultrasonic irradiation to give a magnetic and fluorescent nanohybrid (diameter, 150 nm) with potential applications for the detection of diseases such as tuberculosis or cancer [6]. The effectiveness of the IONP-CD association, as a biosensor for vitamin D2, has been studied by electrochemistry [7]. Due to their optical properties, CDs and IONPs present a highly interesting response in photothermal therapy (PTT). A PTT/PDT application was tested on cancer cells in mice, using a nanomaterial combining magnetite NPs and CDs with graphitic black phosphorus quantum dots [8]. An IONP-CD nanohybrid has also been used in bimodal fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging. The magnetic properties of this nanohybrid were exploited for tissue engineering by static magnetic actuation [9]. (IONP-CD)-based NPs have potential as fluorescent probes capable of delivering chemotherapeutics. In vitro studies show that a model therapeutic, doxorubicin, conjugated onto the NPs, kills cancer cells in a dose-dependent manner [10]. In recent work, the magnetic and optical properties of CDs and IONPs, coupled by thermal treatment, were studied for applications as fluorescent probes in bioimaging and therapy [11].

Thermal therapy has potential in the treatment of cancers [12–14] and bacterial infections [4] and its application to other pathologies such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases is very promising [15]. Heat can be generated in different ways, by magnetic or electric fields, by light or ultrasound. PTT is particularly interesting because it is non-invasive and easy to implement [16]. In this work, CDs and maghemite IONPs were associated in three different ways in order to investigate the effect of the distance between these two entities on the optical and photothermal properties: (i) by peptide coupling using the EDC/NHS procedure, (ii) by Stierglish esterification and (iii) by electrostatic interactions. The elaboration of these nanohybrids, their physicochemical characterization and their photothermal performance under laser irradiation are discussed. The light-to-heat conversion factor depends on the functionalization, and notably depends upon the way that the two types of nanoparticles are combined.

The last part of this study is devoted to testing the antibacterial properties of these nanohybrids for the eradication of planktonic bacteria and biofilms. The World Health Organization has classified infections caused by highly antibiotic-resistant bacteria among its top 10 research priorities. An important reason for bacterial tolerance to antimicrobials is the formation of biofilms, which represent a common life-style for bacteria. Their much higher level of social and spatial organization than that of free planktonic bacteria gives them emerging properties. The formation of biofilms starts when bacteria, evolving freely in an aqueous environment, attach themselves to a surface through physicochemical interactions and specific cell wall decorations (adhesins, pili, capsules). The biofilm matures, increasing in size and spatial organization, thanks to extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) released by the bacteria, that comprise mainly polysaccharides, amyloid proteins and nucleic acids. The life-cycle of the biofilm is completed by the detachment of single cells or small aggregates from the mature biofilm to disseminate in the flow, adhere to a new surface and start a new colony [17].

Given the various nanohybrids that we have synthesized, there are several possible approaches to applying them to the eradication of planktonic and biofilm bacteria. Work on photothermia at relatively low concentrations shows that high temperatures, likely to enhance the bactericidal effect of the nanohybrids, can be achieved. The effect of an oscillating or moving magnetic field goes beyond magnetothermia and may lead to the disruption of cells by mechanical shear forces [18,19]. We explore the impact on bacterial mortality of nanohybrid activation by a magnetic field, and the potential of combining photothermia with magnetic actuation for the eradication of biofilm.

Iron acetate (Fe(CH3CO2)2) 95 % and sodium hydroxide 97.5 % (from VWR); D-(β)-glucose (C6H12O6) 99 %, triethyleneglycol (TEG) 99 %, 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES) 99 %, 1,3-dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide (DCC) 99 %, 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) 99 % (from Sigma Aldrich); methanol, ethanol, 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES), N-hydroxysuccinamide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiamide (EDC) (from Merck). All products were used as received.

CDs were synthesized directly from D-(β)-glucose by one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonication, as reported previously [4]. A 1 M solution of D-(β)-glucose in a 0.5 M aqueous solution of NaOH was ultrasonicated for 4 h at room temperature (frequency 35 kHz, power 100 % (750 W)) using an Elma TI-H-15 sonicator. The colloidal solution was purified overnight using a Float-A-Lyzer G2 dialysis device (MWCO = 0.5–1 kDa). The CD suspension was then stored in the dark at 4 °C. The CDs were used as such in solution or freeze-dried. Particle size was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS).

10−15 nm-diameter (median = 12 nm) maghemite (γ-Fe2O3) IONPs were prepared by adding 1 mL of distilled water to a mechanically stirred solution of 4.3 g of ferrous acetate dissolved in 250 mL of TEG, raising the mixture at 6 °C.min−1 to the boiling point (230 °C) and maintaining it under reflux for 3 h before slow cooling to room temperature. The product was purified by repeated cycles of centrifugation in ethanol, washed with hot water twice, and finally dried at 50 °C for several hours.

NP-APTES was obtained by dispersing 100 mg of as-produced maghemite IONPs in 5 mL of methanol by sonication for a few minutes. The suspension was then diluted in 100 mL of ethanol, and 1 mL of APTES was added. The reaction mixture was mechanically stirred for 1 h at room temperature and then boiled for 2 h under argon. NP-APTES was recovered on a laboratory magnet, washed several times with water and dried at 50 °C for several hours. To produce NP-APTES-CD, 5 mL of a CD suspension (40 μg mL−1) were dispersed in 10 mL MES buffer at pH 6, and NHS and EDC were added to achieve final concentrations of 3.75 mM and 1.5 mM, respectively. The mixture was stirred for 1 h at room temperature. 20 mg of NP-APTES in 2 mL of PBS buffer at pH 9−11 were added to the CD dispersion, and the mixture stirred overnight at 4 °C. The product was collected on a magnet, washed several times with water and ethanol, and dried at 50 °C for several hours.

ce:list-item.ce:para: 50 mL of a CD suspension (40 μg mL−1) were dispersed in 200 mL of dry acetonitrile under argon, and 30 mmol of DMAP and 15 mmol of DCC added successively. To this solution, 50 mg of maghemite IONPs were added, and the mixture stirred overnight at room temperature. NP-ester-CD was collected on a magnet, washed several times with water and dried at 50 °C for several hours.

50 mg of maghemite IONPs were sonicated for a few minutes in 5 mL of methanol. The suspension was then diluted in 200 mL of distilled water, 50 mL of CD suspension (40 μg mL−1) added, and the mixture mechanically stirred overnight at room temperature. The product was recovered on a magnet, washed several times with water and dried at 50 °C for several hours.

ce:section.ce:para: The superficial elemental composition of the nanomaterials was determined on a Thermo VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source at 1486.6 eV; spot size: 650 μm. Standard preparation for XPS measurements was to deposit a few milligrams of the NP powder onto conductive indium tin oxide (ITO) plates.
FTIR transmission spectra at 400–4000 cm−1 were acquired on a Nicolet Magna-IR 860 spectrometer, using the KBr pellet method on samples containing 1 % (w/w) of the nanomaterial (16 scans).

Zeta potential of the nanomaterials were measured on Malvern Nano Zetasizer equipment, using ultrapure water as the background electrolyte. Colloidal aqueous solutions (1 g.L−1) were prepared at pH 7, and ultrasonicated for 10 min before the measurement. DLS measurement of the particle size of CDs was performed under the same conditions.

The JEOL 2100F microscope, featuring a Gatan GIF 2001 for electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), was employed to collect transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) data. In STEM-EELS trials, images were captured with specific settings: spectrum image size of 69 × 98 nm (21 × 30 pixels), spot size set at 1 nm, energy dispersion at 0.5 eV per channel, and a dwell time of 0.3 s per pixel.

Iron concentrations of the nanohybrids were determined by Prussian Blue assay. The complex formed between potassium ferrocyanide and ferric ions, FeIII 4[FeII(CN)6]3, has an absorbance maximum at 705 nm, measured on a Cary 4000 spectrophotometer. A calibration curve (r2 = 0.9973) was made: K4Fe(CN)6 (50 mg) was mixed in 50 mL of a mixture of HCl (0.1 M) and NaCl (0.1 M). A 5 mM solution of iron nitrilotriacetate (FeNTA), source of Fe3+, was added to obtain iron concentrations of 0–0.5 mM. Nanomaterials were first mineralized by adding HCl (37 %) and heating for 5 min at 90 °C. The sample was diluted with the potassium ferrocyanide solution (dilution factor: 100 or 200) and the absorbance recorded.

TGA was performed in air on a Labsys-Evo (Setaram Instrumentation, France) with a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1 up to 800 °C. The weight loss (%) is obtained by tracing two tangents at the inflection points and taking the difference (Calisto-Setaram software), the curve for IONPs being used as the control.

Photothermal (PT) measurements were made on 100 μL aqueous suspensions of CDs, IONPs, NP-APTES, NP-APTES-CD and NP----CD in 0.5 mL polypropylene Eppendorf tubes, with iron concentrations of 5−100 mM. Samples previously stabilized at room temperature were irradiated for 6−8 min by a continuous-wave near-infrared (NIR) laser (Laser Components S.A.S., France) (808 nm, spot diameter 0.3 cm, power 1 W, laser-sample distance 10 cm). The temperature was recorded in real time with an FLIR SC7000 IR thermal-imaging camera.

Tryptic soy broth (TSB) was used as culture medium for Staphylococcus aureus HG003 sGFP and Bacillus Subtilis NDmed bacteria growth. To count the colony-forming units (CFU)/mL, trypticase soy agar (TSA) plates (BioMérieux, France) were used. To visualize the exponential and stationary growth phases, the optical density (OD) of the culture incubated at 37 °C (optimal temperature for bacterial growth) was plotted at regular intervals over time. The exponential phase shows a steep upward curve, while the stationary phase appears as a plateau. To obtain the bacterial culture at the chosen phase, samples were taken at specific time points.

The effect of photothermal heating by nanohybrids on S. aureus and B. subtilis in vitro was investigated. The bacterial culture suspension (50 μL was used alone (9 g L−1 NaCl as control) or put into contact with the nanomaterial suspension. In all such assays, the iron concentration of the nanomaterials was 10 mM. S. aureus and B. subtilis, growing exponentially or in the stationary phase, were incubated with the nanomaterials for 30 min at 37 °C. Samples were then irradiated for 10 min with an 808 nm laser. After treatment, the bacterial residue was dropped onto TSA plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C. Each sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 g in order to prevent further growth of the bacteria. The bacterial pellet was dispersed in 9 g L−1 NaCl, centrifuged again and dispersed under the same conditions. Successive 10-fold dilutions were then made. For each dilution, three drops (10 μL) were deposited on TSA plates, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The bactericidal effect was determined by counting the colony-forming units (CFU) left after treatment and comparing with a control without nanomaterial.

The B. subtilis NDmed used in this work was genetically modified to express the green fluorescent protein (GFP) [20]. A sample was incubated overnight at 30 °C in TSB with 0.1 % of isopropyl ß-d-1-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG). The next day, the bacteria were suspended in a new culture medium with the same concentration of IPTG. In a 96-well plate, 200 μL of the suspension were dropped into each well, and incubated at 30 °C for 2 h to allow the biofilm to adhere and grow. The supernatant was then removed and 200 μL of a mixture of TSB and 0.1 % IPTG were put into the well. The plate was incubated overnight at 37 °C. Then 50 μL of the sample ([Fe] = 10 mM) to be tested or physiological water (for the control) were put into the well, along with 50 μL of propidium iodide (P·I.) for analysis.

Biofilms were observed using a Leica SP8 AOBS inverted laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica Microsystems, Germany) at the INRAE MIMA2 platform [21]. Live bacteria appear green according to the expression of the GFP, and dead cells appear red after reaction with P·I., a cell-impermeant nucleic acid dye (used according to Molecular Probes recommendations) [20]. After 30 min incubation in the dark at 30 °C to enable fluorescent labelling of the bacteria, plates were then mounted on the motorized stage of the confocal microscope. The different compartments selected were scanned using HC PL APO CS2 63x/1.2 water immersion. GFP and P.I. excitation was performed at 488 nm with an argon laser, and fluorescence was recorded in the 500–550 nm and 600–750 nm ranges, respectively, on hybrid detectors. 3D (xyz) acquisitions were performed (512 × 512 pixels, pixel size 0.361 μm, 1 image every z = 1 μm with a scan frequency of 600 Hz). Biofilm biovolume and thickness were extracted from confocal image series using the BiofilmQ software [22].

A 1.4 mT neodymium magnet (20× 10 mm), the top face of which is in contact with the sample well, was displaced over a range of 20 mm, for 5 min at a speed of 4 ± 0.1 mm.s-1, controlled by a 10 V motor. The oscillating magnetic field was combined with PT in a home-made device (Fig. 1 ).

Results are presented as the mean and standard deviation (SD) of 3–24 values for each measurement. The significance of the treatments was tested using a two-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparisons in PRISM software (GraphPad, California, USA). Effects were considered statistically significant when the P-value was less than 0.05.

Maghemite iron oxide nanoparticles were synthesized by the polyol process. One of the great advantages of this process is that the carbonaceous residues on the surface of the particles greatly facilitate their functionalization. Carbon dots were prepared by ultrasound-assisted synthesis from glucose in alkaline medium. IONPs and CDs were combined in three different ways as schematized in Fig. 2 . The first one (NP-APTES-CD) employs the EDC/NHS route, also known as peptide coupling. This method is widely used when a covalent bond is required between primary amine and carboxylic acid functions [23]. APTES is attached to the surface of the IONPs by strong Si–O bonds. Peptide coupling was performed by condensing the amino groups of APTES with the carboxylic acid groups present at the surface of the CDs. APTES acts as a length spacer between the IONPs and the CDs.

The second nanohybrid (NP-ester-CD) was synthesized by DCC/DMAP coupling to perform an esterification between the OH of the IONPs and the COOH of the CDs. Esters are prone to hydrolysis, but are quite stable at physiological pH and ambient temperature [24]. For hydrolysis to occur, water has to access the ester bond; if access is sterically restricted hydrolysis is slowed and the kinetic stability of the ester is increased [25]. By this procedure the NPs are attached to the CDs by short covalent bonds.
The last nanohybrid (NP----CD) was prepared by mixing NPs and CDs mechanically.

The diameters of the IONPs (10–15 nm) and the CDs (2–3 nm) used for the elaboration of the monohybrids were determined by TEM and DLS, respectively (Fig. 3 A). Fig. S1 shows TEM images of NP-APTES, NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD where the particles are spherical and roughly monodisperse. Slight aggregation is observed with IONPs functionalized with APTES. The superficial atomic composition of the nanohybrids was investigated by XPS. Survey spectra are shown in Fig. 3B, the atomic composition by element in Table 1 , and the high-resolution spectra of all elements in Figs. S2–6. The atomic composition of the surface of the CDs (Table 1) shows that it consists mainly of carbon and oxygen, as reported previously [4]. The surface of bare IONPs shows a majority of iron and oxygen, but with a considerable excess of oxygen compared to the atomic composition of maghemite (Fe = 70 wt %, O = 30 wt %). The relatively high carbon content (19 wt %) would therefore appear to correspond to oxygen-rich organic residues remaining after synthesis. The variations in the XPS spectra of the nanohybrids are consistent with the different steps in their functionalization, but the presence of large amounts of carbon on the bare IONPs, as well as in the linkers, makes it impossible to estimate the CD content of the surface.

The Zeta potential (ζ) gives information about the surface charge (Table 1). Its magnitude increases from CDs (−16 mV) and NPs (−17 mV) to attain approximately −30 mV for the three nanohybrids (NP-APTES-CD, −36 mV; NP-ester-CD, −27 mV; NP----CD, −31 mV) which indicates good colloidal stability for all the latter.

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to follow the modification of IONPs by CDs. Fig. 3C shows the IR spectrum, from 400 to 4000 cm−1, of bare IONPs and those associated with CDs: NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD; characteristic frequencies and corresponding chemical functions are reported in Table S1. In the low-frequency region, around 580 and 630 cm−1, there are strong bands in all samples due to Fe–O vibrations in maghemite. In NP-ester-CD a peak around 1725 cm−1 corresponds to the ester CO vibration. The bands around 2850–2950 cm−1, corresponding to aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations, are more apparent in NP-APTES-CD because of the carbon skeletons of APTES and of the CDs. These bands appear also in the other samples but are weaker, and probably arise from CDs and residual organics at the surface of bare IONPs (see below). Similarly, all samples show bands ranging from 1000 to 1200 cm−1, attributed to C–O stretching. In the high-frequency range, around 3400 cm−1, there is a broad band corresponding to –O–H. This band is stronger in NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD, but also features in bare IONPs. The FTIR spectra do not provide an unambiguous signal characteristic of CDs [4]. For this reason, other techniques were employed in order to establish their presence on the surface of the nanohybrids.

The amount of organic matter was estimated by default, i.e., by determining the Fe(III) content by a classical wet analytical method, based on the formation of the Prussian Blue complex (see Experimental section). The results (Table 2 ) indicate that bare IONPs contain some 6.6 % of material which is not maghemite, and is assumed to be residual water and organic matter, with the latter predominant. Quantities of non-maghemite materials are greater for the functionalized particles, consistent with the addition of organics, either APTES or CDs or both. In the work on photothermia (see below) the amounts of IONPs or hybrids required to obtain the stated Fe concentrations were based on the maghemite contents determined by this assay (Table S2).

TGA allows to determine the quantity of water and organic matter, either volatile or likely to be decomposed into volatile components at high temperature, in the hybrids. TGA was performed at 25–800 °C, with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in air. The TGA curves are presented in Fig. S7, and the corresponding values, referenced to bare IONPs and taking into account the non-maghemite content of the latter (6.6 %) are reported in Table 2 (see Experimental section). Relative to bare IONPs, the weight losses were 6.9%, 4.3% and 4.0% for NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD, respectively.

Another means of measuring the organic content of the nanohybrids is to use vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) to determine the saturation magnetization (Msat) which falls as the maghemite is diluted by the organics (Table 2 and Fig. 3D). The superparamagnetic behavior of the NPs and the nanohybrids is consistent with their size and nature [26]. By referencing the Msat values of NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP----CD to that of NP, and by assuming the latter's Prussian Blue value for the maghemite content (93.4 %), values for the other materials are calculated (Table 2). Values of the non-maghemite content obtained by Prussian Blue analysis, magnetometry and TGA, all referenced to bare IONPs, are in good agreement and increase as expected with their progressive functionalization. They indicate that the three CD-containing nanohybrids all contain approximately the same proportion of CDs, about 4 % (Table 2).

For the applications of photothermal therapy (PTT) in humans, it is interesting to work in one of the three biological windows (700–1800 nm), where absorption by water and hemoglobin is low [27]. Near-Infrared (NIR) light (600–900 nm) penetrates the skin (2–4 mm) further than any other wavelength on the “light” spectrum, which gives it a considerable advantage for wound healing, the long-term objective of the present work.

The photothermal (PT) properties of CDs and maghemite NPs have been established [4,12,28]. One of the aims of this study is to see whether there is a synergy between these properties when CDs and NPs are combined in nanohybrids, and to investigate the structural dependence of any such effect. Samples of IONPs, CDs, NP-APTES, NP-APTES-CD, NP-ester-CD and NP-CD in aqueous suspension at iron concentrations from 5 to 100 mM were exposed to continuous NIR laser irradiation at 808 nm, and temperature versus time (T/t) curves recorded by an IR camera. Upon irradiation the temperature increases until a plateau is reached, and decreases rapidly when the laser is turned off, as presented for [Fe] = 20 mM suspensions, as an example (Fig. 4 ).

Values of the maximal increase in temperature, ΔT = Tmax – Ta, with Ta the ambient temperature and Tmax the temperature reached at the end of laser irradiation are listed in Table S3. Under the same conditions the temperature increases more for NP-APTES-CD than for IONPs alone or any other composite thereof. NP-APTES does not perform significantly better than raw IONPs in this range. NP-ester-CD and NP----CD behave very similarly and both are more efficient than NP-APTES. Similar experiments were performed on CDs in order to determine their contribution to the temperature elevation. Considering that the CD content in any nanohybrid is around 4% (Table 2), concentrations were calculated to correspond to their amounts in the nanohybrids at the Fe concentrations given. CD concentrations corresponding to [Fe] = 5, 20, 70 and 100 mM were 0.006, 0.0024, 0.082 and 0.118 mg mL−1, respectively. In this concentration range the photothermal response goes from 4 to 6 °C (Table S4 and Fig. S8).

The specific absorption rate (SAR) and the photothermal conversion coefficient (η) express the photothermal capacities of these materials (see Supporting Information Text S1. Photothermal capacities). At very low concentrations the SAR values are uniformly high (Table 3 ), with those for the nanohybrids bearing CDs the highest. As the Fe concentration increases by a factor of 20, all values fall by a factor between about 10 and 20 on going to the highest concentration, but the SAR remains about twice as great for the CD-containing nanohybrids as for those without. However, this cannot be attributed simply to the photothermal response of the CDs. For the CDs alone at the appropriate concentrations the SAR values are considerably lower, going from 185 to 14 as the concentration increases (Fig. S9A), and would clearly contribute very little to the overall response if they were simply mixed with the IONPs. Even for NP----CD, where the two types of nanoparticles are held together by electrostatic interactions, the impact of their presence is almost as great as in the two other hybrids where they are chemically bonded.

Values of the light-to-heat conversion coefficient
η ,
given in Table S5 and presented graphically in Fig. 5 , show that the suspensions of IONPs and NP-APTES present a low light-to-heat conversion coefficient (between 20 % and 30 %), increasing slightly with the concentration [29,30]. On the other hand, for metallic nanoparticles associated with CDs, there is a marked increase in
η
with the Fe concentration. When this is raised from 30 mM to 100 mM,
η
increases by 44% for the NP----CD suspension, by 53% for NP-ester-CD and by 61% for NP-APTES-CD. This result cannot be explained by the small additional absorption of the CDs in the IR range [31,32] which, moreover, would not account for the differences between the nanohybrids, which all contain approximately the same amount of CDs. Values of
η
for CDs go from about 0.04 to 0.08 (Fig. S9B). The way in which the CDs are attached to the IONPs has a significant effect upon the SAR and
η
values, the APTES spacer being more effective than ester bonding or direct attachment. It is tempting to suggest that this is related to the geometry of the hybrid, with the NP⋅⋅⋅⋅CD distance increasing from NP----CD to NP-ester-CD to NP-APTES-CD. Nevertheless, it remains that all the CD-containing nanohybrids show a structure-dependent synergy between the CDs and the IONPs. In previous work, a photothermal conversion coefficient of 20–30 % was considered enough to justify the use of such materials in antitumoral targeted thermal therapies [12,30,33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties of nanohybrids on planktonic S. aureus and B. subtilis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the efficiency of the nanohybrids, we selected two Gram-positive bacteria: Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 12600 and Bacillus subtilis NDmed. S. aureus is responsible for food poisoning, skin infections, endocarditis and lethal pneumonia [35,36]. It has a broad spectrum of resistance to antibiotics, and also thermal resistance [37,38]. The processes of this resistance are complex and call for new treatment strategies. B. subtilis is a GRAS bacterium (generally recognized a safe) and is the model species used to dissect the genetic regulation of Gram-positive biofilm. In addition, the B. subtilis NDmed strain used in this study, isolated from an endoscope washer disinfector in the UK, forms a slimy biofilm hyper-resistant to biocides and protects S. aureus from the action of antimicrobials in bispecies biofilms [20,39].

The effect of photothermal heating by nanohybrids on S. aureus and B. subtilis in vitro was investigated. The log(CFUs/mL) of S. aureus in the exponential and stationary phases, after contact with the nanohybrids is represented in Fig. 6 (A, B), and the values of the Δlog(CFU/mL) in Table S6. We will consider in this study that a 3-log or higher reduction indicates a strong bactericidal effect relevant to medical applications [40]. The statistical significance of any effect is determined by ANOVA variance analysis. No relevant effects were observed without PT. When PT was applied to bacteria in the exponential phase, the treatments were statistically significant (P < 0.05), the most effective treatment being that with NP-APTES-CD. When PT is applied to cells in the stationary phase, the functionlized NPs exhibit the highest reductions (P < 0.05), with NP-APTES-CD achieving a 2-log reduction (99%), and NP-ester-CD and NP----CD between a 1- and 2-log reduction (90–99%).
The log(CFU/mL) of B. subtilis in the exponential and stationary phases, after contact with the nanohybrids is represented in Fig. 6 (C, D), and the values of the Δlog(CFU/mL) in Table S7.

In the absence of PT, the bactericidal effect is very low for any of the nanomaterials in either phase, as for S. aureus. When PT is applied to bacteria in the exponential phase, NP-APTES-CD has the best bactericidal effect, achieving a 3 to 4-log reduction (P < 0.05). NP-ester-CD and NP----CD show log reductions between 2 and 3 (P < 0.05). For bacteria in the stationary phase subjected to PT, the most effective nanohybrid is NP-APTES-CD again, with, as in the exponential phase, a 3 to 4-log reduction (P < 0.05), while NP-ester-CD and NP----CD achieve reductions around 2-log (P < 0.05).

It is commonly observed with antimicrobials that bacterial cells tend to exhibit reduced sensitivity during the stationary stage of growth. This phenomenon is attributed to the lower metabolic activity of cells in this phase, coupled with the heightened expression of various stress tolerance systems. This dynamic underscores the importance of considering the distinct physiological states of bacterial populations when evaluating the efficacy of antimicrobial agents.
These results clearly demonstrate the effectiveness of PT in the NIR region at 808 nm, and show that B. subtilis is more sensitive to the nanohybrids than S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm properties of NP-APTES-CD on B. subtilis biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: The proof of concept for the treatment of biofilm infections was established using NP-APTES-CD, the nanohybrid with the highest antibacterial effect, and B. subtilis biofilm. With the genetically modified strain used, live cells appear green (GFP) and dead cells red (P·I.) (sections 2.7.3-4). The biofilm was subjected to different conditions: (i) to an oscillating magnetic field, (ii) to PT and (iii) to the combination of the two in a home-made device (section 2.7.5). Analysis of confocal microscopy images of the biofilm (Fig. 7 A), provides quantitative parameters that help to understand its spatial organization [41]. The mortality parameter corresponds here to the proportion of dead cells in the biofilm (Fig. 7B) [42].

Under the experimental conditions described, if neither laser irradiation nor magnetic actuation is applied, the mortality is below 6−7 %, regardless of whether any nanohybrid is present or not. When a magnetic field is applied the biofilm is partially destructured (Fig. 7). This treatment results in low but statistically significant mortality rates compared to the control: 12 % for IONP (P < 0.05) and 20 % for NP-APTES-CD (P < 0.05). In the presence of NP-APTES-CD, PT causes 50 % mortality (P < 0.05), the values being lower for IONP and NP-APTES at around 30 % (P < 0.05), while that for the control sample is about 10 % (Fig. 7). There is a remarkable effect when the biofilm is treated with NP-APTES-CD and both magnetic actuation and PT are applied simultaneously, the resulting mortality of 90 % being greater than would be obtained by the addition of PT and magnetic actuation alone (P < 0.05). This dual therapy has little or no impact on the efficacy of treatment with IONP or NP-APTES, where the mortality is slightly less than with PT alone.

Image analysis from confocal microscopy provides other relevant metrics concerning the biofilm structure: biovolume and height. The biovolume represents the volume of bacteria per unit of surface and is expressed in cubic micrometers. Data for B. subtilis biofilms are presented in Fig. 8 . With PT and dual treatment, for all nanomaterials, the biovolume is significantly lower than for the control (P < 0.05). This is because laser irradiation heats the water surrounding the particles, causing it to evaporate quickly, resulting in expanding and imploding water vapor nanobubbles. Moreover, heating to a sufficiently high temperature reduces the biofilm yield stress, which means that the matrix is more fluid than without treatment [43]. This results in a flattening of the biofilm, which is more pronounced with PT alone. The biovolume is slightly lower in a moving magnetic field due to the fact that the magnetic particles generate shear forces which displace the biofilm, create holes and may kill some bacteria (Fig. 8A).

The biofilm height represents the maximum thickness of the biostructure and is expressed in micrometers above the substratum. B. subtilis biofilm heights increase markedly with magnetic actuation (P < 0.05) compared to controls, under PT or not (Fig. 8B). This suggests that the movement of the nanoparticles, generated by the magnetic field, disrupts the biofilm matrix, leading to a thicker but less dense film. In contrast, PT alone without magnetism induces a slight but not statistically significant decrease in biofilm thickness (P > 0.05) that can be associated with a slight increase of temperature.

With PT treatment, for all nanomaterials, the biofilm heights are lower than for the control (P < 0.05), a phenomenon which can be attributed to the increased fluidity of the matrix resulting from the increase in temperature.

Magnetic actuation alone is not associated with high mortality, but the decrease in the density of the biofilm caused by magnetic destructuration makes the bacteria more accessible and facilitates their eradication by other treatments. This result is consistent with previous studies where static magnetic fields were used to channel drug-carrying magnetic nanoparticles though biofilms. These results provide valuable insights for the clinical development of antimicrobial-carrying magnetic nanoparticles [44,45]. When PT is applied alone, the mortality is higher, but the matrix heats up and compacts the biofilm, reducing access to the bacteria. The dual treatment, combining the advantages of magnetic actuation and PT, results in the highest mortality.

Among the three nanohybrids elaborated, NP-APTES-CD shows the highest performance in photothermia and magnetic actuation, and can be considered as a potential agent for the eradication of biofilm. Our strategy to fight against biofilms is based on: (i) generating local heat by PT to weaken the biofilm matrix; (ii) creating shear forces by means of a mechanically oscillated magnetic field to disturb the biofilm organization; (iii) coupling magnetic actuation and PT in dual magneto-photothermia to destruct biofilms and kill planktonic bacteria. This cooperative effect between magnetic actuation and PT under our conditions leads to a 90 % mortality of living biofilm cells that was not attained with a single treatment. Treatments which disrupt the biofilm matrix resensitize biofilm cells to antibacterials are effective against their planktonic homologs. The results of the present work suggest that further therapeutic enhancement could be achieved by grafting drugs onto nanohybrids for targeted delivery by irradiation, magnetic actuation and other stimuli. This study, therefore, opens perspectives for the development of multistimulable nanomaterials to allow multitherapy in the treatment of bacterial biofilms and chronic infections, a major scientific and medical challenge.

Célia Sahli: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Julien Deschamps: Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Laurent Royon: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. John S. Lomas: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Romain Briandet: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Miryana Hémadi: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Ecole Chimie Physique et Chimie Analytique de Paris Centre (ED388) of the Université Paris Cité for a research grant (for C.S.) and the ANR (Agence Nationale de la Recherche) and the CGI (Commissariat à l’Investissement d’Avenir) through IDEX and Labex SEAM (Science and Engineering for Advanced Materials and devices): ANR-18-IDEX-0001(MANO4T Emergence project), ANR 11 LABX 086 and ANR 11 IDEX 05 02, for financial support. The authors are grateful to Dr Philippe Decorse for the XPS spectra measurements on the XPS-UPS facility which is supported in part by the Ile-de-France regional council, SESAME agreement n° 16016303, and to Alexandre Chevillot-Biraud for the TGA. The authors are grateful to the French Antibiodeal network of the Promise PPR antibioresistance ANR program for stimulating scientific exchanges.
